royal-portraits.talk: You address the assembled monarchs. 'Any advice?' King Aldric the Bold stares blankly. Queen Thessaly the Wise offers nothing. King Mortimer the Prepared -- well, he wasn't prepared for an olive, so his counsel is suspect.
royal-portraits.open: These are portraits, not cupboards. You push one aside, expecting a secret compartment. Just wall. The dynasty's secrets are limited to poor life choices and questionable interior design.
royal-portraits.push: You push a portrait. It swings out slightly, revealing... more wall. A century of dust cascades onto your head. The portrait settles back into place.
royal-portraits.pull: You pull a portrait toward you. The wire groans, the frame tilts, and dust rains down. The king depicted looks amused. Or constipated. Hard to tell with oil paint.

locked-door.take: You attempt to remove the door from its hinges through sheer willpower. The door, being a door, remains exactly where doors are supposed to be.
locked-door.talk: You knock politely. 'Hello? Anyone in the kitchen?' The silence from beyond suggests either nobody's home, or the kitchen staff have a very strict policy about answering doors.
locked-door.pull: You grab the handle and pull. The door is locked, not ajar. Pulling a locked door is optimism in its purest and most futile form.

cursed-portrait.open: Open a painting? It's not a book. It's not a door. It's a cursed portrait of a dead queen, and it is emphatically not interested in being opened. The glow pulses irritably.
cursed-portrait.push: You reach toward the frame. The green glow flares brighter as your hand approaches, the temperature dropping perceptibly. Every survival instinct you possess suggests reconsidering. You reconsider.
cursed-portrait.take: You reach toward the cursed portrait. The green glow flares and your fingers go numb. The portrait does not wish to be relocated. The portrait's wishes are enforced by magic.

columns.talk: You whisper to a column. 'Holding up all right?' It is. It has been for centuries. It will continue to do so long after you've been forgotten. Columns are good at perspective.
columns.open: Columns are solid stone. There is nothing to open. If you're hoping for a secret passage, you've been reading too many adventure novels.
columns.push: You shove a column. It doesn't move. It weighs several tons and is holding up a castle. Your contribution to its structural analysis is noted and dismissed.
columns.pull: You try to pull a column toward you. This is the architectural equivalent of trying to persuade a mountain to relocate.

hallway-carpet.talk: You address the carpet. It does not respond. Centuries of being trodden on have given it a stoic disposition and absolutely no interest in conversation.
hallway-carpet.open: You lift a corner of the carpet. Underneath: stone floor, dust, and the fossilized remains of someone's optimism. No secret passages.
hallway-carpet.push: You try to push the carpet aside. It's nailed down and weighs roughly the same as your ambitions -- heavy, unwieldy, and going nowhere fast.
hallway-carpet.pull: You grab an edge and pull. A cloud of dust erupts with the fury of centuries. The carpet moves approximately one inch. A pyrrhic victory at best.

wall-sconces.talk: You address the flames. They flicker. This could mean anything, from 'I acknowledge your existence' to 'I am fire and do not have opinions.'
wall-sconces.open: Sconces don't open. They hold fire. That's their entire professional remit. They're not underperforming -- you're over-expecting.
wall-sconces.push: You push a sconce bracket. It pivots slightly, and for one thrilling moment you think you've found a secret lever. Then it springs back. It was just loose.
wall-sconces.pull: You yank a sconce. The bracket bends, the flame sputters indignantly, and a shower of rust flakes rains down. The sconce returns to its original position with weary resignation.
